perturbation.original_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder). The term "Confidential Information" does not include information which information and its relevance in the particular instance (A) is a matter of common knowledge or public record, (B) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of disclosure by the Stockholder, (C) which information and its relevance is generally known in Business, (D) the Stockholder can demonstrate that such information and its relevance in the applicable instance, was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation of the Stockholder under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non-disclosure agreement, or (E) the information is required to be disclosed by law or pursuant to court order or subpoeona.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the exceptions to what constitutes confidential information, the agreement now provides no clear safe harbor for pre-existing knowledge or legally compelled disclosures. This potentially exposes the Stockholders to liability even when disclosing legally required information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the exception provided by the DTSA, which protects individuals from liability for disclosing trade secrets in confidence to a government official or attorney solely for the purpose of reporting or investigating a suspected violation of law, or in a court filing made under seal. By removing condition E, the Stockholder would be in violation of the agreement even if mandated by law or court order to disclose. This contradicts the protections offered by the DTSA.

Exceptions to prohibitions (a) In General .-This chapter does not prohibit or create a private right of action for- (1) any otherwise lawful activity conducted by a governmental entity of the United States, a State, or a political subdivision of a State; or (2) the disclosure of a trade secret in accordance with subsection (b). (b) Immunity From Liability for Confidential Disclosure of a Trade Secret to the Gov
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes an exception to the prohibition of disclosing confidential information, specifically the exception for disclosures required by law or court order. The Law Explanation correctly states that by removing condition E, the Stockholder would be in violation of the agreement even if mandated by law or court order to disclose. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1833, refers to "Exceptions to prohibitions", which implicitly acknowledges that prohibitions, such as those outlined in the original contract, may have legally recognized exceptions. The removal of exception E directly contradicts this understanding, because it provides no exception when such exceptions are acknowledged to exist. The change creates a direct conflict with the DTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 7. THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED (BOTH AS TO VALIDITY AND PERFORMANCE) AND ENFORCED IN ACCORDANCE WITH AND GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE APPLICABLE TO AGREEMENTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY WITHIN SUCH JURISDICTION.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Material Agreement. (a) It is understood by and between the parties hereto that the foregoing -2- <PAGE> covenants by the Stockholders are essential elements of this Agreement and that but for the agreements of the Stockholders to comply with such covenants, AUGI would not have entered into the Merger Agreement or paid the Merger Consideration to the Stockholders. AUGI and the Stockholders have independently consulted with their respective counsel and have been advised concerning the reasonableness and propriety of such covenants with specific regard to the businesses conducted by the Company. (b) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary, express or implied, contained in this Agreement, the Merger Agreement or in any other "Transaction Document" (as that term is defined in the Merger Agreement), no future act of omission or commission by AUGI or any of its affiliates, or other actual or alleged breach by AUGI or such affiliates of any of their covenants or agreements contained in any Transaction Documents (in addition to this Agreement), shall in any way be deemed to modify, limit or terminate the covenants and agreements of the Stockholders contained in Section 1 and Section 2 hereof, all of which shall remain in full force and effect in accordance with their respective terms. 7. THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED (BOTH AS TO VALIDITY AND PERFORMANCE) AND ENFORCED IN ACCORDANCE WITH AND GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF WASHINGTON APPLICABLE TO AGREEMENTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY WITHIN SUCH JURISDICTION.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law is moved to a less noticeable location and changed to the State of Washington which might not be readily apparent to someone reviewing the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles / Delaware General Corporation Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The initial agreement specifies Delaware law. Changing this to Washington law introduces a conflict. This may be problematic because the contract involves Delaware corporations (AUGI, Mergerco, and the Company). Delaware law grants corporations broad freedom to contract, but this shift may complicate enforcement if Washington law differs on relevant issues, especially if internal affairs of a Delaware corporation are implicated. The bylaws might conflict with the changed state.
perturbation.location: Section 3. Material Agreement and 7. Choice of Law

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Delaware law, while the changed text specifies Washington law. The Law Explanation clarifies that the contract involves Delaware corporations, and changing the governing law could complicate enforcement, particularly if Washington law differs on relevant issues concerning the internal affairs of a Delaware corporation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 outlines aspects of Delaware corporation law, indicating that these provisions would be relevant to the contract. The Perturbation Explanation also notes that the location of the governing law was moved to a less noticeable spot in the contract, which is a structural flaw. Therefore, this creates a structural flaw that introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Without intending to limit the remedies available to AUGI or the Company, each Stockholder further agrees that damages at law will be an insufficient remedy to AUGI if such Stockholder(s) breaches the terms of this Agreement and that AUGI or the Company may apply for and have injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to restrain the breach or threatened breach of or otherwise to specifically enforce any of the covenants contained herein, in each case without the necessity of proving damages or posting bond.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which is in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law shall be deemed, if possible, to be modified or altered to conform thereto or, if not possible, to be omitted herefrom. The invalidity of any portion hereof shall not affect the force and effect of the remaining valid portion hereof. 4. Without intending to limit the remedies available to AUGI or the Company, each Stockholder further agrees that damages at law will be an insufficient remedy to AUGI if such Stockholder(s) breaches the terms of this Agreement and that AUGI or the Company may apply for and have injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to restrain the breach or threatened breach of or otherwise to specifically enforce any of the covenants contained herein, in each case without the necessity of proving damages or posting bond.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the equitable relief clause after the severability clause, one might argue that if a statute requires a bond for injunctive relief, the severability clause overrides the specific agreement waiving the bond requirement. This creates ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 65(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 65(c) generally requires security (a bond) for a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order. The contract states that AUGI or the Company may seek injunctive relief 'without the necessity of proving damages or posting bond.' However, the preceding severability clause could be interpreted to mean that if a court finds the waiver of the bond requirement to be in conflict with FRCP 65(c), the waiver is deemed modified or omitted, potentially undermining AUGI's ability to obtain injunctive relief without posting a bond.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces a potential contradiction regarding the necessity of posting a bond for injunctive relief. The original text explicitly waives this requirement. However, the added severability clause preceding this waiver could be interpreted to mean that if a statute (like FRCP Rule 65(c), as suggested by the Law Explanation) requires a bond, the waiver is deemed modified or omitted. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Rule 65, which pertains to Injunctions and Restraining Orders. While the snippet doesn't explicitly detail the bond requirement itself, the Law Explanation suggests a conflict with FRCP 65(c). This creates ambiguity about whether a bond is required, undermining the original intention to waive it, thus leading to a structural flaw resulting in a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Material Agreement. (a) It is understood by and between the parties hereto that the foregoing -2- <PAGE> covenants by the Stockholders are essential elements of this Agreement and that but for the agreements of the Stockholders to comply with such covenants, AUGI would not have entered into the Merger Agreement or paid the Merger Consideration to the Stockholders. AUGI and the Stockholders have independently consulted with their respective counsel and have been advised concerning the reasonableness and propriety of such covenants with specific regard to the businesses conducted by the Company. (b) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary, express or implied, contained in this Agreement, the Merger Agreement or in any other "Transaction Document" (as that term is defined in the Merger Agreement), no future act of omission or commission by AUGI or any of its affiliates, or other actual or alleged breach by AUGI or such affiliates of any of their covenants or agreements contained in any Transaction Documents (in addition to this Agreement), shall in any way be deemed to modify, limit or terminate the covenants and agreements of the Stockholders contained in Section 1 and Section 2 hereof, all of which shall remain in full force and effect in accordance with their respective terms.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Material Agreement. (a) It is understood by and between the parties hereto that the foregoing -2- <PAGE> covenants by the Stockholders are essential elements of this Agreement and that but for the agreements of the Stockholders to comply with such covenants, AUGI would not have entered into the Merger Agreement or paid the Merger Consideration to the Stockholders. AUGI and the Stockholders have independently consulted with their respective counsel and have been advised concerning the reasonableness and propriety of such covenants with specific regard to the businesses conducted by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing subsection (b) eliminates the clause that prevents AUGI's future actions from modifying the Stockholders' non-compete and confidentiality obligations. This makes the enforceability of the non-compete and confidentiality clauses more susceptible to arguments that AUGI's conduct constituted a waiver or modification of those obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing subsection (b), the contract becomes more susceptible to arguments based on the doctrine of waiver. The principle of waiver dictates that a party can voluntarily relinquish a known right. Without the clause explicitly preventing AUGI's actions from modifying the Stockholders' obligations, a court could find that AUGI's subsequent conduct (e.g., failing to enforce the non-compete in similar cases, disclosing confidential information) constitutes a waiver of its right to enforce those provisions against the Stockholders. This undermines the intended strict enforceability of Sections 1 and 2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a provision that prevented AUGI's actions from modifying the stockholders' obligations, creating susceptibility to a waiver argument, as explained in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1 defines waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege' and that it 'can also be implied through actions'. Without the clause explicitly preventing AUGI's actions from modifying the Stockholders' obligations, it could be argued that AUGI's conduct constitutes a waiver of its right to enforce those provisions.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which is in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law shall be deemed, if possible, to be modified or altered to conform thereto or, if not possible, to be omitted herefrom. The invalidity of any portion hereof shall not affect the force and effect of the remaining valid portion hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. This Agreement shall become effective upon the effectiveness of the Merger and shall terminate prior to the expiration of the Restrictive Period only with the written consent of AUGI. 6. Any paragraph, sentence, phrase or other provision of this Agreement which is in conflict with any applicable statute, rule or other law shall be deemed, if not possible, to be modified or altered to conform thereto or, if not possible, to be omitted herefrom. The invalidity of any portion hereof shall not affect the force and effect of the remaining valid portion hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the severability clause to state that a provision is only modified if it is not possible, then omitted, it might be argued that even if there is a minor conflict, the entire provision is removed, rather than amended to conform.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Severability Clause Interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Typical severability clauses aim to preserve as much of the contract as possible. By stating that a conflicting provision is *omitted* if modification is *not possible*, the clause deviates from the intent to preserve the contract. Courts generally prefer to modify contracts to conform to the law where feasible, rather than striking down entire provisions. This modification creates a scenario where even minor conflicts could lead to the removal of entire clauses, contrary to standard interpretation principles. Therefore if it might have been easily modified, under contract law, the whole clause should not be removed. This removal deviates standard contract interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification to the severability clause creates a legal contradiction. The original text implies an attempt to preserve the contract where possible, as is typical. However, the changed text states that if modification to conform with the law is *not possible*, the provision is omitted. This deviates from standard contract interpretation that favors modifying to conform rather than complete removal. Snippet 1, § 2-302, supports this as it states that the court may 'enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result.' The changed text undermines this principle by prioritizing omission over modification, even for minor conflicts.
